ontology.analysis_summary: Singapore's NAIS 2.0 outlines a systems-based approach to harness AI for public good, focusing on three systems: Activity Drivers (Industry, Government, Research), People & Communities (Talent, Capabilities, Placemaking), and Infrastructure & Environment (Compute, Data, Trust). The 100 Experiments programme exemplifies industry-academia collaboration with co-funding. Key actors include AI Researchers (Creators), AI Practitioners, Enterprises, Government Agencies, and AISG as the coordinating body. Resources flow through grants and compute allocation, while eligibility criteria and outcome reports govern participation and evaluation.

Through 100E, Q&M Dental Group's EM2AI has developed an AI model that helps dentists, and has deployed this across its clinics in Singapore and Malaysia.

source_spans.text: AI Singapore (AISG) is the national programme office coordinating AI research, talent, and industry collaboration.
The 100 Experiments programme exemplifies industry-academia collaboration with co-funding, managed by AISingapore.
We have committed more than S$500 million through AI Singapore (AISG) under the RIE 2020 and 2025 plans.
We have committed more than S$500 million through AI Singapore (AISG) under the RIE 2020 and 2025 plans.
We will encourage greater experimentation and collaboration, focusing on interesting and impactful challenges, to reap greater benefits from AI.
AISG helps pair companies with public researchers to develop AI solutions that address the former's problem statement, and provides co-funding of up to S$330,000 per project.
Q&M Dental Group worked with AISG to create an AI model.
Through 100E, LightSpeed Studios is partnering with AISG to develop a new text-to-speech system for gamers.

In February 2023, AISG and the Republic of Korea (ROK)’s Institute for Information & Communication Technology Planning and Evaluation (IITP) launched a joint grant call to fund research on AI-based net-zero energy building management optimisation systems.
Self-assessment tools like AISG's AI Readiness Index (AIRI) are useful in helping companies identify and map out the capabilities and infrastructure they need for using AI.

source_spans.text: Enterprises are end-users and demand drivers for AI solutions.
Recent advances, especially in Generative AI, have opened doors to new possibilities for enterprises to adopt AI.
We must invest in our people, enterprises, and digital infrastructure.
This requires investments in our people, enterprises, and digital infrastructure, so that we can create new jobs and participate in industries of the future.
We shall uplift and empower our people and businesses, equipping them with the capabilities and resources to thrive in an AI-enabled future.
A confident base of enterprises and workers can use AI tools.
A confident base of enterprises and workers can use AI solutions.
Industry, Government, and Research drive AI activity, from basic science to product R&D and adoption.
Businesses were given free access to Google Cloud's AI toolsets for up to three months.
Many companies have already embarked on AI-enabled innovation projects, which are often focused on the Application layer.
We will work with Industry Champions to identify sector use cases and crowd in a broader base of researchers and companies in the sector.
Research breakthroughs can translate into commercial products, which can be used by our companies.
Their research breakthroughs can also translate into commercial products, which can be used by our companies and across government agencies to realise better outcomes.
100 Experiments (100E) is AISG's flagship programme, aimed at solving AI problem statements proposed by industry where no commercial off-the-shelf AI solutions exist.
Several companies, from a range of sectors, have participated in the 100E programme.
The project also drew in industry partners from both countries.
AI has the potential to help enterprises increase their productivity and stay relevant, provided they have the capabilities to apply it well.
Empower enterprises and workers to adopt AI and stay ahead of the curve.
Singapore will intensify the promotion of AI adoption across all enterprises.
We will promote baseline digital adoption for our enterprises and enhance the enterprise digitalisation toolkit, to support more sophisticated AI adoption.
For more digitally mature enterprises, we will provide tailored support for AI-enabled business transformation.
The vibrancy of the community in turn attracts the participation of even more talented individuals, companies, and capital.
The data concierge can explore facilitating handshakes between private industry entities as a trusted intermediary.
The Government will also elevate the security and resilience baseline for all system owners using AI.

Their research breakthroughs can also translate into commercial products, which can be used by our companies and across government agencies to realise better outcomes.
This will allow Singapore to level up our current capabilities by working with the best in the world and make a meaningful contribution to global AI development.

source_spans.text: Singapore believes in the transformative potential of AI. Our second National AI Strategy represents Singapore's commitment to realise the benefits of AI.
The report sets out government plans to build a thriving AI ecosystem, develop workforce, provide infrastructure, and foster a trusted environment.
In Singapore, where the vast majority of our people already frequently interact with digital technologies to transact with the Government and businesses.
Singapore believes that AI can be a potent force for good, to uplift human potential.
The recent breakthroughs in AI demand a renewed focus by Singapore to refine our national strategies for AI.
Singapore's second National AI Strategy (NAIS 2.0) will drive the next bound of our Smart Nation journey.
NAIS 2.0 starts with the conviction that we must do our utmost to harness AI for the Public Good, for Singapore and the World.
Singapore intends to provide more platforms which can bring our AI community together.
Singapore must ensure reliable, localised access to high-performance compute, so that industry, academia, and the Government have the means to innovate and build here.
Singapore is determined to play our part, and we will continue to participate constructively at international fora on AI.
Singapore is determined to play our part, and we will continue to participate constructively at international fora on AI.
Singapore is committed to being a serious and reliable international partner on AI.
Singapore demonstrates commitment to developing and deploying AI well, and will leverage these gains to be a global pace-setter.
We will actively profile Singapore's approaches to AI through public engagements and conferences such as Asia Tech x Singapore and SCAI.
Singapore’s NAIS 2.0 is therefore designed to unfold as a national movement on the global stage.
Published 4 December 2023 Copyright © 2023 Government of the Republic of Singapore

source_spans.text: For governments, these developments have raised new and critical questions around the risks and responsibilities associated with the development and use of such a powerful technology.
We will ensure that Singapore hosts the necessary infrastructure and provides a trusted environment for AI innovation.
The Government has a key role in curating the right incentives, resources, regulatory frameworks, and partners.
The Government has a strong track record of managing such nation-wide transitions through our policies and outreach.
For Singapore, this will involve: Availing compute and data for AI innovation.

The Government will support research and development for PETs, especially in targeted areas like synthetic data generation, data annotation, federated learning, and homomorphic encryption, and expand practical measures like regulatory sandboxes and guidelines to promote experimentation with PETs.
The Government will continue to maintain a regulatory environment for AI that is pro-innovation while ensuring appropriate guardrails.
The Government will also elevate the security and resilience baseline for all system owners using AI.
The Government will also elevate the security and resilience baseline for all system owners using AI.

source_spans.text: In 2022, we also launched AI Verify, an AI governance testing framework and software toolkit, and made it open source for developers in June 2023.
Singapore’s initiatives, like AI Verify, have been welcomed as pragmatic contributions to the growing body of work on AI governance.
We will regularly review and adjust frameworks like the Model AI Governance Framework and AI Verify to reflect emerging principles, concerns, and technological developments.
AI Verify is an AI governance testing framework and software toolkit developed by IMDA.
We have developed governance frameworks and open-source testing toolkits such as AI Verify.

dropped_nodes.name: GovernmentAgency

source_spans.text: Government agencies are key actors in orchestrating AI adoption across public services.
Recent advances, especially in Generative AI, have opened doors to new possibilities for government agencies to deploy AI.
Address risks from the potential abuse and mismanagement of AI. These include the misuse of AI to amplify harms across society, especially toward vulnerable groups.
Industry, Government, and Research drive AI activity, from basic science to product R&D and adoption.
Government agencies were given free access to Google Cloud's AI toolsets for up to three months.
Agencies can also attend to cases more quickly.
Government agencies equipped with specialised knowledge, technical capabilities, and regulatory tools will develop and lead sector-specific AI strategies.
All Government agencies that lead such functions will identify and optimise specific business lines with AI, and build internal capabilities to deliver business outcomes.
Facilitating cross-agency data sharing, providing access to high-performance compute and associated engineering capabilities, and issuing facilitative policy guidelines.
Their research breakthroughs can also translate into commercial products, which can be used by our companies and across government agencies to realise better outcomes.
Through programmes like SMEs Go Digital and CTO-as-a-Service (CTOaaS), the Government has helped businesses gain awareness of suitable AI-enabled solutions.
This includes leveraging IMDA and EnterpriseSG’s Digital Leaders Programme (DLP).
Community-building events drew in start-up founders, investors, and government agencies.
The Government can consider stepping in for selected high-value use cases where we can play a catalytic role.
The Government will also establish a common platform for regulatory agencies to coordinate on AI developments in their sectors, and share best practices when governing AI.
